frontmatter.title: Creative AI
frontmatter.subtitle: An open textbook for a bachelor course at the University of Oslo

children.value: You are reading the open textbook for
, a bachelor-level course at the University of Oslo (UiO). The course is open to students from

faculties, regardless of prior experience with programming, machine learning, or any particular art form. The book is also written for self-study and as a public resource for anyone curious about creative uses of AI.
The aim of the course is twofold:
of how today’s generative AI systems are built and why they behave the way they do.
using these systems for creative work, alongside the critical vocabulary needed to discuss their cultural, ethical, and environmental consequences.
Having completed the course, you will be able to:
describe in plain language how modern generative AI systems (large language models, diffusion models, audio and video models) work, including what they cannot do;
use AI tools to produce text, images, sound, video, code, and other artefacts, and reflect on what is gained and lost in the process;
discuss ethical, legal, and environmental questions raised by Creative AI — copyright, bias, labour, energy, authorship, and authenticity;
design and document a small creative project that uses AI deliberately and responsibly.
The course runs for 12 teaching weeks. Each week has

, plus around 6 hours of self-study and project work. Practice sessions are deliberately mixed: some weeks you will use commercial tools in the browser, other weeks you will write a few lines of Python in a notebook on your laptop or in
A working definition; brief history; scope of the course
First experiments with one text and one image tool; reflective journal entry
Neural networks and learning from data
Train a tiny model in a notebook; look inside a pretrained model
The vocabulary shared by all generative systems
Compare outputs from different samplers and prompts
Large language models for writing and dialogue
Build a prompt library for your discipline; evaluate hallucinations
From noise to picture; control via prompts and references
Make a four-image series with a controlled variable
Generate and remix a 30-second clip; document the prompt chain
Storyboard and produce a 10-second clip; critique it as a viewer
Pair programming with an LLM; from sketch to running code
Build a small interactive sketch with an AI coding assistant
Generative pipelines for spatial and game content
Generate a 3D asset or game element; integrate into a scene
Design (on paper) a multi-step AI agent for a creative task
Ethics and politics of Creative AI
Class debate; ethical audit of a tool you have used
Where Creative AI may go; project showcase
Speculative design; sustainability and creative practice
Present your final project; peer feedback
Each chapter is roughly the length of one short lecture’s worth of reading. The end of every chapter has a
section — that is what you will work on in the 2-hour session that week — and a
section with suggested readings, tools, and links.
A course for everyone at UiO

children.label: A course for everyone at UiO

children.value: a specialist course in computer science, art, or media. It is designed as a

course: students arrive from law, medicine, musicology, design, theology, mathematics, dentistry, education, literature, biology, and many other places. That diversity is the point. The classroom is itself a small interdisciplinary laboratory where lawyers and artists, medics and historians can ask hard questions of the same AI tool and watch each other’s answers.

, but the practice sessions provide an optional “code track” for students who want to look under the hood. Where we use code, it is in Python notebooks that you can run in your browser without installing anything.
Active learning and a flipped classroom

children.label: Active learning and a flipped classroom

children.value: . Each weekly cycle is structured as:
— Short talk, demonstrations, and class discussion of the week’s ideas.

— A guided hands-on session in the lab or seminar room. You will work alone or in pairs with real AI tools, and produce a small artefact (a paragraph, an image, a sound, a sketch, a short video, or some code).
The textbook is meant to be read
the lecture, not in place of it. The lecture is the place to argue, demo, and answer the questions you bring in.
course: the content rests on current research from machine learning, human–computer interaction, media studies, the humanities, and the arts. It is also
: the teachers are themselves working on Creative AI projects, and parts of the course (in particular the final-project showcase) feed into ongoing research at UiO.
A note on AI tools used to write this book

children.label: A note on AI tools used to write this book

children.value: This textbook is itself an example of AI-supported authorship. Drafts of every chapter have been written collaboratively with large language models, then revised, fact-checked, and re-organised by human editors. Where AI tools have produced figures or examples, we say so. We treat the book as a living document — please open an issue or a pull request on the
when you spot errors or omissions.
Across the semester you will meet many tools. The list below is not exhaustive, and any of it may change as the field moves. The point is to
, so that you can evaluate the next tool that appears.
— running open-weight models locally on your laptop

Specific products listed above will appear, merge, and disappear during the semester. Treat the list as a starting point, not a syllabus. In every practice session we will use whatever currently works well enough for the task at hand.
The textbook follows the principles of

: the material is openly licensed (CC-BY-4.0), the source is on GitHub, and we point to open tools and datasets where possible. Where a tool requires a paid account, we say so and try to offer an open alternative.
This is also a political stance. Creative AI is being built and deployed mainly by a handful of large companies. Treating the
of Creative AI as an open, collaborative project is one small way to push back.
Assessment in this course consists of:
— a short reflective entry each week documenting what you did, with what tool, what you noticed, and what questions it raised. The logs are graded pass/fail.
— a small creative project produced over the second half of the semester, presented in week 12, plus a short written reflection. Group projects (2–3 students) are encouraged.
There is no traditional written exam. The expectation is that you can talk and write coherently about what you made and why.
to use AI tools in this course. You must, however,
. A simple log of prompts and decisions is enough. Hiding AI use is treated as academic dishonesty; using AI thoughtfully and transparently is treated as a course goal.

The chapters are roughly linear, but they are also self-contained. If you are a complete beginner, read in order. If you already have some background, you can skim chapters 2–3 and jump into the application chapters that interest you (text, image, sound, video, code).
Each chapter follows the same shape:
— the ideas you should be able to explain after reading.
— what you will do in the 2-hour session.
